title: AP World History: Modern SAQ Practice Exam 01

questions.prompt:
Source 1: Historian Lin Wei, “Commercial Life in Song China,” modern historical interpretation, 2018.
“The Song period should be understood as a commercial transformation driven not only by rulers but also by merchants, farmers, artisans, and urban consumers. Expanding markets, paper money, printed materials, and growing cities encouraged people to participate in a more connected economy than earlier Chinese dynasties had experienced.”
Source 2: Historian Amelia Grant, “States and Markets in Medieval East Asia,” modern historical interpretation, 2021.
“Song China’s economy expanded because the state created conditions that made market growth possible. Government investment in transportation, tax collection, agricultural improvement, and examination-based administration gave merchants and producers a more stable framework in which to operate.”
(a) Identify one difference between the two historians’ interpretations of economic change in Song China.
(b) Describe one specific historical development in Song China that could be used to support one of the interpretations.
(c) Explain one way economic developments in Song China were similar to or different from economic developments in another region during c. 1200–1450.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 3
(a) 1 point: Identifies a clear difference between the interpretations, such as Source 1 emphasizing bottom-up commercial activity while Source 2 emphasizes state support and administrative structures.
(b) 1 point: Describes a relevant Song development, such as paper money, Champa rice and agricultural expansion, urbanization, printing, merchant networks, internal waterways, or examination-based bureaucracy, and connects it to one interpretation.
(c) 1 point: Explains a valid similarity or difference with another region, such as the Dar al-Islam, Swahili Coast, Indian Ocean world, Europe, or South Asia, using a specific economic development and a clear comparison.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong answer should not merely state that Song China “had trade.” It should explain whether economic growth was driven more by social-commercial activity, state policy, or both. For part (c), the strongest responses name another region and compare a specific development, such as merchant diasporas, urban markets, trade routes, state infrastructure, or monetized exchange.

questions.prompt:
Source: Memorial from Chen Liang, provincial governor in Sichuan, to the Qing imperial court, Beijing, 1713. This excerpt is original but modeled on the style of early eighteenth-century Qing administrative memorials.
“The people of the newly registered districts have begun to submit grain taxes, but many families remain unsettled after years of disorder. I request permission to repair the roads used by the courier stations and to appoint clerks who can translate between local speech and the language of the banner officers. If Your Majesty grants temporary tax relief to the poorest households, they will see that the dynasty governs with benevolence. The garrisons must remain alert, yet harsh punishments alone cannot secure loyalty. Local scholars should be invited to assist in schools and ritual observances so that the people may learn the proper order of ruler and subject.”
(a) Identify the likely purpose of the governor’s memorial.
(b) Explain how the memorial reflects one challenge faced by land-based empires during c. 1450–1750.
(c) Explain one way the policies described in the memorial reflect broader methods used by land-based empires to maintain power.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 3
(a) 1 point: Identifies a plausible purpose, such as persuading the emperor to approve tax relief, infrastructure repair, translation, local cooperation, or a balanced policy of military control and benevolent rule.
(b) 1 point: Explains a relevant imperial challenge, such as governing diverse populations, integrating frontier regions, maintaining loyalty after conquest, collecting taxes, coordinating military and civilian administration, or managing linguistic and cultural differences.
(c) 1 point: Explains a broader method of imperial rule, such as using bureaucracy, military garrisons, infrastructure, tax policy, religious or ideological legitimacy, local elites, education, or cultural accommodation.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong response should use the source’s point of view and purpose. The governor is writing upward to the imperial court, so he has an incentive to present his recommendations as useful for order and loyalty. Strong answers connect the Qing example to wider land-based imperial strategies rather than treating the memorial as only a local problem.

questions.prompt:
Source: Description of a colonial railway and telegraph map of India, produced by an engineering office in Calcutta, 1885.
The map shows thick railway lines connecting Bombay, Calcutta, and Madras to inland regions labeled “cotton,” “jute,” “wheat,” “coal,” and “military cantonments.” Telegraph lines run beside many of the railway routes. Major port cities are marked with large circles, while many rural districts away from export crops or troop stations have few or no railway connections. A legend states: “Railways completed, railways under construction, telegraph lines, principal export commodities, troop stations.”
(a) Identify one economic purpose of the railway and telegraph network shown in the described map.
(b) Explain how the map reflects the relationship between technology and imperialism during c. 1750–1900.
(c) Explain one social or economic effect that railway and telegraph construction could have had on people living in colonized regions.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 3
(a) 1 point: Identifies a valid economic purpose, such as moving raw materials to ports, linking export zones to global markets, transporting coal or commercial crops, reducing shipping time, or improving communication for trade.
(b) 1 point: Explains that industrial technologies such as railways and telegraphs helped imperial states extract resources, coordinate administration, move troops, connect colonies to metropolitan economies, or strengthen political control.
(c) 1 point: Explains a valid effect on colonized peoples, such as increased commercialization of agriculture, migration for labor, disruption of local economies, new employment opportunities, tighter colonial control, famine vulnerability due to export priorities, or expanded nationalist communication networks.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong response should treat the map as evidence of colonial priorities. The placement of railways near ports, crops, coal, and troop stations matters. Strong answers may also note that technologies could serve imperial extraction while unintentionally helping colonized people circulate ideas, organize politically, or migrate for work.
